text: The surgeon introduces a reusable transanal DAP device into the anus to treat a benign rectal stricture in a patient with a visible rectal ulcer.
The surgeon applies a silicon cap to facilitate the use of a 10mm, 30-degree regular length scope alongside the reusable curved DAPRI instrument during the anal surgery.
The camera view shows the benign rectal stenosis clearly visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon introduces a monocurve reusable grasping forceps through the port's orifice at the nine o'clock position, visible within the anal surgical field.
A measuring straight grasper is introduced through the ports orifice at a three o'clock position, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon confirms the lesion is located 2.5 centimeters away from the anal margin, visible through the transanal approach.

The surgeon inserts a monocurve reusable coagulating hook through the port at the three o'clock position to begin the procedure, with the surgical field visible through the silicon cap using a 10mm, 30-degree scope.
The surgeon uses a needle to create a circumferential tattoo around the visible benign stenosis, marking the area for further surgical intervention.
The surgeon initiates dissection by making a mucosal opening at the six o'clock position, extending laterally on both sides and then moving anteriorly.
The surgeon carefully pushes the muscle layer laterally using a coagulating hook, ensuring precise manipulation during the procedure.
The surgeon reveals the submucosal plane posteriorly, following the initial mucosal opening at the six o'clock position.
The surgeon continues the submucosal dissection cranially while preserving the mucosal layer, which remains visible at the bottom of the surgical field.
The surgeon preserves the muscle layer laterally, connecting the previous submucosal dissection both anteriorly and posteriorly, ensuring the muscle layer remains intact.
The surgeon continues the dissection posteriorly, revealing well-exposed muscular fibers as the submucosal plane becomes more defined.
The surgeon continues the dissection laterally and anteriorly, maintaining the preservation of the mucosal layer at the bottom while progressing cranially.
The final view of the dissection reveals the introduction of the coagulating hook into the mucosal lumen, navigating over the stenosis.
The surgeon cuts the distal margin of the dissected mucosa, completing the separation of the tissue in the surgical field.
The surgeon removes the specimen through the D-port, revealing the full length of the stenosis.
The surgeon controls the entire muscular layer and the distal mucosal margin, ensuring proper alignment and repair of the dissected area.
The surgeon tests the feasibility of suture repair on the dissected area, assessing the tissue's ability to hold sutures effectively.
The surgeon introduces a monocurve reusable needle holder through the port orifice at the three o'clock position, inserting the needle through the central port orifice.
The surgeon begins the repair by placing the first suture at the six o'clock position on the surgical site.
The surgeon passes the suture through the inferior edge and then into the superior edge of the tissue, continuing the repair process.
The surgeon performs intercorporeal sutures and knots within the anal canal, maintaining ergonomic positions and demonstrating freedom of movement interluminally.
The surgeon inserts monocurve reusable scissors through the port's orifice at the three o'clock position and cuts the first suture.
The surgeon places another suture at the twelve o'clock position, using a monocurve reusable needle holder introduced through the port orifice.
The surgeon inserts the needle into the superior edge after passing the suture into the inferior edge, and performs an intercorporeal knot to secure the repair.
The surgeon places another suture at the two o'clock position, using precise technique to secure the tissue.
The surgeon continues the operation, demonstrating smooth hand movements and coordination without any visible conflict between the hands during the procedure.
The final closure of the surgical site is displayed, showing the completed suturing with no visible gaps or bleeding, marking the end of the procedure.
